column5: This sheet serves as the MASTER LIST of all indicators proposed for the dashboard. It defines how indicators are organised within the dashboard structure.

column7: Definition (what the indicator measures)
column8: Relevance (why it matters for the project)
column12: Method (calculation or derivation)

column7: Measures the percentage of children aged 0-59 months whose height-for-age Z-score falls below -2 standard deviations from the WHO Child Growth Standards, indicating stunting.
column8: This indicator captures chronic undernutrition and long-term dietary deprivation in early childhood. It is central to understanding whether the food system supports sustained access to diverse and nutritionally adequate diets during the first 1,000 days of life.
column12: (Number of children 0–59 months with HAZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column7: Measures the percentage of children aged 0-59 months whose weight-for-height Z-score falls below -2 standard deviations from the WHO Child Growth Standards, indicating wasting.
column8: This indicator reflects acute undernutrition and short-term food insecurity. It is particularly relevant for monitoring how shocks to food access, affordability, and availability affect child nutritional status.
column12: (Number of children 0–59 months with WHZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column7: Measures the percentage of children aged 0-59 months whose weight-for-age Z-score falls below -2 standard deviations from WHO Child Growth Standards, indicating underweight status.
column8: This indicator provides an overall measure of child undernutrition by capturing elements of both chronic and acute nutritional deprivation. It helps assess whether the food system is providing sufficient and nutritious food for healthy child development.
column12: (Number of children 0–59 months with WAZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column6: Prevalence of obesity and overweight for under 5
column7: Measures the percentage of children aged 0-59 months whose weight-for-height Z-score exceeds +2 standard deviations from WHO Child Growth Standards, indicating overweight or obesity.
column8: This indicator highlights early exposure to unhealthy dietary patterns and food environments. It helps track the nutrition transition and the extent to which the food system promotes or undermines healthy child diets.
column12: (Number of children 0–59 months with WHZ > +2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column7: Measures the percentage of women aged 15-49 years with a Body Mass Index below 18.5 kg/m2, indicating underweight status.
column8: This indicator reflects inadequate dietary intake and constrained food access among women of reproductive age. It is important for assessing equity within the food system and the implications of poor nutrition for maternal and intergenerational health.
column12: (Number of women with BMI < 18.5 / Total number of women measured) × 100

column6: Prevalence of obesity and overweight for women
column7: Measures the percentage of adult women classified as overweight or obese using the Asia-standard threshold of BMI greater than or equal to 23 kg/m2, reported as an age-standardized estimate.
column8: This indicator captures the diet-related non-communicable disease risk associated with unhealthy food environments and rising consumption of energy-dense foods. It helps assess whether the food system supports healthy dietary outcomes for women.
column12: (Number of women with BMI ≥ 23 / Total number of women measured) × 100

column7: Planned indicator to measure the minimum cost of a diet that meets basic energy needs at the lowest possible price.
column8: This indicator will help assess whether staple dietary needs can be met at a cost that is feasible for low-income households, providing a baseline measure of diet affordability.
column12: Data not available yet

column6: Cost and affordability of healthy diets
column7: Planned indicator to measure the cost of a diet that meets recommended nutritional requirements and to assess whether that diet is affordable for different population groups.
column8: This indicator will support analysis of whether households can access diets that are not only sufficient in calories but also diverse and nutritionally adequate.
column12: Data not available yet

column7: Measures the spatial accessibility of food outlets based on walking distance from population clusters to nearby outlets.
column8: This indicator shows how easily residents can reach food outlets on foot and helps identify neighbourhoods where physical access may constrain healthy food choices.
column12: Calculated by measuring walkable distance from population locations to the nearest food outlets within a defined buffer
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the density of healthy food outlets within each commune, expressed relative to land area.
column8: This indicator shows how protective food environments are distributed across the city and helps identify areas with stronger access to healthier food options.
column12: Calculated as the number of healthy outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the density of unhealthy food outlets within each commune, expressed relative to land area.
column8: This indicator identifies areas where obesogenic food environments are concentrated and where residents may face greater exposure to unhealthy food choices.
column12: Calculated as the number of unhealthy outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the density of mixed food outlets within each commune, expressed relative to land area.
column8: This indicator captures food environments where healthy and unhealthy options coexist, helping to identify more complex or mixed dietary landscapes.
column12: Calculated as the number of mixed outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the balance between unhealthy and healthy food outlets within each commune, expressed as a ratio.
column8: This indicator summarizes the relative dominance of unhealthy versus healthy outlets and helps identify locations where the food environment may place residents under greater obesogenic pressure.
column12: Calculated by dividing the number of unhealthy outlets by the number of healthy outlets in each commune.
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the proportion of residents who live within a walkable distance of healthy food outlets.
column8: This indicator shows the share of the population with practical access to healthier food options and helps identify spatial inequalities in supportive food environments.
column12: Calculated as the proportion of the population living within the walkable buffer of healthy outlets
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Measures the proportion of residents who live within a walkable distance of unhealthy food outlets.
column8: This indicator captures the extent of population exposure to unhealthy food environments and helps identify areas where obesogenic food access is widespread.
column12: Calculated as the proportion of the population living within the walkable buffer of unhealthy outlets
column14: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column6: Living standard dimension (housing condition, assets, drinking water, electricity, cooking fuel, sanitation)
column7: Measures the percentage of households deprived in the living standards dimension of the OPHI Multidimensional Poverty Index, including housing conditions, assets, drinking water, electricity, cooking fuel, and sanitation.
column8: This indicator helps identify population groups experiencing structural deprivation that may constrain food access, diet quality, and resilience within the food system.
column12: Global MPI index used by UNDP and Oxford Public Health Institute
column14: Update depend on the release/access of new survey
column18: Galina shared with me, when I joined the project

column7: Measures the percentage of households deprived in the health dimension of the OPHI Multidimensional Poverty Index, including nutrition and child mortality.
column8: This indicator helps identify populations facing overlapping health and poverty disadvantages that are closely linked to food system inequities and nutrition outcomes.

column7: Measures the percentage of households deprived in the education dimension of the OPHI Multidimensional Poverty Index, including years of schooling and school attendance.
column8: This indicator supports analysis of structural disadvantage by highlighting education-related deprivation, which shapes long-term livelihood opportunities, dietary access, and resilience.

column7: Tracks the presence or absence of national binding policy documents relevant to food systems, including policies on biofortification, food environments, nutrition labelling, and the marketing of unhealthy foods to children.
column8: This indicator provides an overview of the policy environment shaping the food system and helps assess the extent to which governance frameworks support healthier, more sustainable, and more equitable diets.
column12: FAOLEX dataset consultation, based on keywords, and confirmation by summary reading.

column7: Compiles a structured database of organisations, institutions, and other actors involved in the food system.
column8: This indicator supports stakeholder mapping, coordination, and analysis of the governance landscape by showing who is active in the food system and where influence and implementation capacity are located.

column6: Rice flow estimations from Vietnam regions to Hanoi, by rural and urban areas, in tons per year (2010-2022)
column7: Estimates the annual flow of rice from producing regions in Vietnam to Hanoi, including rural and urban consumption, expressed in tonnes per year.
column8: This indicator helps quantify Hanoi's dependence on external staple supply and identifies the regional production linkages that underpin urban food security and vulnerability to supply disruption.
column12: Trade attractiveness method, including two steps as follows: A) Estimation of Rice Net Supply (Consumption – Production) for every province, based on Consumption (Population * Consumption per person), and Production (Paddy production/Live weight/Raw production * Conversion rate). B) Distribute the rice consumption of Hanoi, considering: Province Production, National Production, and International Import.
column15: Based on estimations, depending on consumption trends

column6: Life cycle assessment of food items
column7: Estimates the environmental footprint of selected food items using life cycle assessment methods.
column8: This indicator supports analysis of sustainable diets by showing how different foods compare in terms of environmental impact across the supply chain.

column7: Measures how well cropland vegetation maintains healthy vegetation conditions during agricultural drought events.
column8: This indicator helps identify production areas that are most vulnerable to climate stress and where agricultural shocks are most likely to affect wider food supply chains.
column11: Internally produced from ERA5-Land and MODIS
column12: Calculated using the vegetation condition index and the SPEI6 medium-term agricultural drought classification system to assess vegetation condition relative to drought severity.
column15: Land cover classification accuracy of active cropland

column7: Measures anomalies in total terrestrial water storage, including groundwater, surface water, and soil moisture, relative to expected seasonal conditions.
column8: This indicator is important for understanding water-related constraints on agricultural production and for identifying regions where water stress may undermine food system resilience.
column12: Calculated by removing the climatology and seasonality from the signal to reveal only anomalies from the usual seasonal water cycle.
column14: Can only update when CSR updates

column7: Summarizes the frequency of reported natural disasters at national scale using the EM-DAT disaster database.
column8: This indicator provides a historical record of exposure to biophysical shocks and helps contextualize resilience risks within the broader hazard environment.

column7: Measures the resilience of urban food commodity price systems through a composite index combining volatility persistence and the frequency of extreme price shocks.
column8: This indicator captures the ability of food markets to absorb and recover from shocks. It is useful for understanding whether urban food systems remain stable when exposed to price volatility.
column9: Unitless (0-1) Dimensionless composite score, normalised 0–1 within commodity group (0 = least resilient, 1 = most resilient)
column11: World Food Programme Vulnerability Analysis and Mapping (WFP VAM) database monthly wholesale food prices, 120 Ethiopian markets
column12: GARCH(1,1) models estimated on monthly log-return series (rₜ = ln(Pₜ/Pₜ₋₁)) using an ARMA(1,1) mean equation via the rugarch package in R. Persistence component = 1 − (α₁ + β₁). Shock frequency component = 1 − (proportion of months exceeding 95th percentile cross-commodity threshold). Both components normalised to [0,1] within commodity group. UFPRI = arithmetic mean of the two normalised components.
column15: Pulse UFPRI scores are preliminary due to short data windows (19–20 observations); shock frequency component is unreliable for pulses and driven entirely by persistence. Cereal and pulse scores are not directly comparable (normalised separately).

column7: Measures the percentage of district area occupied by different land-cover categories, from aquaculture and cropland to urban land and forest cover.
column8: This indicator helps identify where urban and peri-urban production or other land-cover assets may buffer the food system against supply shocks and support broader resilience planning.
column12: Area reducer applied to LULC masks
column15: LULC product hasn't been updated since 2020

column7: Measures the proportion of adults aged 18 years and older with diabetes, based on fasting glucose, treatment status, or prior diagnosis, reported as an age-standardized estimate.
column8: This indicator captures a major diet-related non-communicable disease outcome and helps assess how food environments and dietary patterns are shaping long-term health risks.

column7: Measures the proportion of adults aged 18 years and older with hypertension, defined as systolic and/or diastolic blood pressure greater than or equal to 140/90 mmHg, reported as an age-standardized estimate.
column8: This indicator reflects the health consequences of dietary patterns associated with excess salt, processed foods, and inadequate fruit and vegetable consumption. It helps connect food system conditions to non-communicable disease risk.

column7: Measures the number of recorded food poisoning cases.
column8: This indicator captures the food safety dimension of the food system by reflecting failures in food handling, storage, processing, or regulation that affect safe access to food.

column7: Planned indicator covering environmental quality dimensions relevant to food systems, including water and air quality.
column8: This indicator is intended to capture environmental conditions that shape food production, food safety, and public health, and will be developed further as data availability is confirmed.
